answers.value:
B.A. in Sociology, University of Colorado Boulder, 2001
Certificate in Nonprofit Management, University of Denver, 2010
Extensive A.A. service training through district and area workshops (GSR, DCM, and Delegate orientation sessions)
Ongoing professional development in organizational leadership, strategic planning, and diversity/equity/inclusion through various nonprofit conferences (2012–2024)
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
I attended my first A.A. meeting in Denver, Colorado, on 2006-04-18, the day after my sobriety date. My home group for most of my sobriety has been the "Capitol Hill Primary Purpose" group in Denver, where I have been a committed member since late 2006. Over the past 18 years, I have held a wide range of group, district, and area service positions.

Group Level:
- Early Service Positions (2007–2010): I started by making coffee, setting up chairs, and greeting at the door. I then served as Group Literature Chair (2008–2009), where I became familiar with A.A. Conference-approved literature and helped our group maintain an inventory that met the needs of newcomers and long-timers alike. I also served as Treasurer for one term (2009–2010), implementing a simple but transparent reporting system and helping the group understand our 7th Tradition contributions to the district, area, and G.S.O.
- General Service Representative (GSR) (2011–2013): I served two consecutive terms as GSR for my home group. During this time, I regularly attended district meetings, area assemblies, and pre-conference workshops. I reported back to the group on agenda items, Conference topics, and area business, and facilitated group consciences on issues such as literature projects, safety in A.A., and the use of technology in carrying the message.

District Level:
- District Committee Member (DCM) (2014–2016): I served as DCM for District 13 in Area 10 (Colorado). In this role, I chaired monthly district meetings, coordinated with GSRs, and worked closely with district committee chairs (CPC, PI, Corrections, Treatment, and Grapevine/La Viña). We focused heavily on improving communication between groups and the area, increasing participation in district activities, and supporting new groups in underserved neighborhoods. I helped organize two district-wide workshops on “Service Sponsorship” and “Anonymity and Social Media,” which were well-attended and later adapted for use at the area level.
- District CPC/PI Chair (2017–2018): After rotating out as DCM, I served as the district’s Cooperation with the Professional Community and Public Information Chair. I coordinated presentations to local hospitals, treatment centers, and a few large employers that had Employee Assistance Programs. I worked with our area CPC committee to ensure our messaging aligned with A.A. guidelines while remaining accessible and clear. We developed a simple, one-page resource sheet for professionals that highlighted what A.A. is and is not, with local contact information and links to aa.org.

Area Level (Area 10 – Colorado):
- Area Treatment Committee Member (2015–2016): While serving as DCM, I also sat on the area Treatment Committee. I participated in developing a set of guidelines for bridging the gap between treatment and A.A., helping to coordinate volunteers who could accompany newcomers from facilities to their first outside meetings.
- Area Delegate, Panel 70 (2020–2022): I was elected as Area 10 Delegate and served a full two-year term. I attended the General Service Conference in 2020 and 2021 (including the pandemic-related virtual Conference) and participated in discussions and advisory actions on topics such as literature revisions, the use of technology in carrying the message, and efforts to reach underrepresented communities. I prepared detailed written and verbal Conference reports for the area, visiting districts across Colorado (both in-person and virtually) to share Conference outcomes and encourage participation in the Conference process. I learned firsthand how A.A. World Services, A.A. Grapevine, and the General Service Board interact, and gained experience reviewing background material, participating in committee work, and reaching an informed group conscience.

Service Beyond the Area:
- Regional Forums and Workshops: I have attended several West Central Regional Forums and Regional Service Assemblies, where I have had the opportunity to interact with trustees, A.A.W.S. directors, and G.S.O. staff. I have participated in sharing sessions about the future of A.A. literature, digital communication, and participation of younger members.
- Service Sponsorship and Mentoring: Over the last decade, I have actively service-sponsored several members, helping them navigate roles such as GSR, district chair, and DCM. I emphasize the difference between personal opinion and an informed group conscience, and I try to model calm, principled participation in sometimes-heated discussions.

Overall, my A.A. experience has given me a broad exposure to the Fellowship at the group, district, area, and Conference levels, as well as frequent contact with G.S.O. staff, trustees, and board members. I have a solid working understanding of the A.A. Service Manual, the Twelve Concepts for World Service, and the practical realities of implementing spiritual principles in organizational decision-making.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
I have over 20 years of professional experience in nonprofit management, program development, and organizational leadership, primarily in the fields of behavioral health, homelessness services, and community development.

Key Roles:
1. Case Manager, Front Range Recovery Center, Denver, CO (2002–2007):
   - Worked directly with adults in early recovery from alcohol and substance use disorders, helping them coordinate treatment, housing, employment, and legal obligations.
   - Collaborated regularly with clinical staff, probation officers, and local treatment providers.
   - Gained firsthand experience with the continuum of care and the ways A.A. interacts (and sometimes does not interact) effectively with treatment systems.

2. Program Coordinator, Mile High Housing & Recovery Collaborative, Denver, CO (2007–2013):
   - Coordinated a coalition of nonprofits providing housing, mental health, and recovery support services to people experiencing homelessness.
   - Managed small grants (ranging from $25,000–$150,000), tracked outcomes, and reported to funders.
   - Facilitated monthly interagency meetings, building consensus among organizations with different missions and priorities.
   - Developed and implemented a data collection system (using a basic CRM) to track client outcomes, which improved reporting accuracy and helped secure renewed funding.

3. Director of Programs, Colorado Community Wellness Network (CCWN), Denver, CO (2013–2019):
   - Oversaw a portfolio of community-based programs focused on mental health, addiction recovery support, and peer-led initiatives.
   - Supervised a team of 12–15 staff, including program managers, peer specialists, and administrative personnel.
   - Led strategic planning sessions with the executive director and board, helping to align programming with the organization’s mission and financial realities.
   - Managed an annual program budget of approximately $2.5 million, including federal, state, and private foundation grants.
   - Implemented outcome measurement and evaluation processes, and worked closely with our finance department to ensure compliance and transparency.

4. Executive Director, Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance (DRRA), Denver, CO (2019–Present):
   - Currently serve as the chief executive of a mid-sized nonprofit that operates peer-run recovery community centers, training programs for peer coaches, and community education initiatives.
   - Responsible for overall organizational leadership, including strategic direction, fiscal oversight, board relations, fundraising, and staff development.
   - Work closely with a volunteer board of directors to set policy, approve budgets, and conduct long-term planning.
   - Oversee an annual operating budget of approximately $4.1 million, with revenue from government contracts, private grants, corporate sponsorships, and individual donations.
   - Navigate complex stakeholder relationships, including funders, community partners, and people with lived experience of addiction and recovery.

Throughout my career, I have gained substantial experience in:
- Governance: Serving as staff to nonprofit boards, preparing board packets, presenting financial and programmatic updates, and supporting board committees (finance, governance, and development).
- Finance and Operations: Working with CFOs and controllers to develop and monitor budgets, interpret financial statements, and manage risk; implementing internal controls and policies.
- Human Resources and Organizational Culture: Hiring, training, performance evaluation, and conflict resolution; promoting inclusive and trauma-informed workplaces.
- Strategic Planning and Change Management: Leading organizations through periods of growth, funding shifts, and external crises (including the COVID-19 pandemic).

This combination of direct service, program management, and executive leadership has given me a practical understanding of how mission-driven organizations function, make decisions, and remain accountable to the people they serve—experience I believe is highly relevant to serving on the A.A. World Services Board.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board
answers.value:
Professional Skills and Experience:
1. Governance and Board Relations:
   - For the past decade, I have regularly worked with nonprofit boards as a senior staff leader and now as an executive director. I prepare board reports, develop meeting agendas in collaboration with board officers, and support board committees. I have experience clarifying roles and boundaries between governance and management, which I understand is also a key principle in the relationship between the A.A. General Service Board, its corporate boards, and the General Service Office.
   - I have participated in board self-assessments and strategic planning retreats, helping boards evaluate their effectiveness, understand their fiduciary responsibilities, and remain aligned with organizational mission and values.

2. Financial Literacy and Organizational Stewardship:
   - I am comfortable reading and interpreting financial statements (balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow reports). In my current role, I work closely with our finance director to monitor budget vs. actuals, manage reserves, and ensure we are prepared for fluctuations in funding.
   - I have experience developing and monitoring multi-year budgets, including scenario planning and risk assessment. I understand the importance of prudent, conservative financial management in service to a mission, as opposed to purely growth-driven thinking.

3. Strategic Planning and Evaluation:
   - I have led or co-led several strategic planning processes for nonprofits, including environmental scans, stakeholder interviews, and the development of measurable goals and indicators.
   - I bring an evaluation mindset that balances quantitative data (numbers, usage statistics, financial trends) with qualitative feedback (stories, sharing sessions, listening to minority voices). I believe this balance aligns well with A.A.’s tradition of listening carefully to the Fellowship while also paying attention to practical realities.

4. Technology and Communication:
   - While not an IT professional, I am comfortable with digital tools, remote meeting platforms, and basic data systems. During the pandemic, I led our organization’s transition to virtual services, and I was deeply involved in how our A.A. area adapted to online and hybrid meetings.
   - I have thought a great deal about anonymity, safety, and accessibility in online settings, and I have helped facilitate group and district discussions about how to use technology without compromising A.A.’s spiritual principles.

Personal Background and Life Experience:
1. Long-Term Sobriety and Service:
   - With sobriety since 2006-04-17, I have experienced A.A. through multiple life stages: as a newcomer in my late twenties, as a parent and professional navigating career changes, and as a middle-aged member watching how A.A. evolves over time. I have both benefited from and contributed to A.A. at many levels.
   - I sponsor several men, most of whom are balancing recovery with work, family, and sometimes legal or financial challenges. These relationships keep me grounded in A.A.’s primary purpose and remind me that all service, including at the world level, ultimately comes back to one alcoholic helping another.

2. Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI):
   - As a Latino man who grew up in a working-class family, I have personal experience with some of the barriers people face in accessing help, including language, stigma, and lack of representation.
   - Professionally, I have been involved in DEI initiatives, including staff trainings on cultural humility and efforts to make organizational leadership more reflective of the communities served.
   - In A.A., I have participated in sharing sessions and informal discussions about how to better reach communities that are underrepresented in our meetings, including people of color, younger alcoholics, and those with co-occurring mental health challenges.

3. Communication and Conflict Resolution:
   - I am a careful listener and a reasonably calm presence in difficult conversations. In both A.A. and my professional life, I have often been asked to facilitate or mediate when there are disagreements about direction, policy, or personalities.
   - I am comfortable holding minority opinions when necessary, but I also know how to support group conscience decisions even when they do not align with my personal preferences. Serving as an Area Delegate during controversial agenda items taught me how to carry a truly informed conscience rather than a personal agenda.

4. Understanding of A.A. Structure and Principles:
   - My experience as a GSR, DCM, and Delegate has given me a working knowledge of A.A.’s structure, the Twelve Concepts for World Service, and the relationship between spiritual principles and practical administration.
   - I understand that A.A. World Services, Inc. exists to serve the Fellowship and to carry the message through literature, services, and communication—not to direct or govern A.A.

Languages and Cross-Cultural Experience:
- I am bilingual in English and Spanish (conversationally fluent), which has allowed me to support Spanish-speaking newcomers in Denver and to occasionally help with informal translation at meetings and district events.
- I have attended Spanish A.A. meetings both locally and in Mexico while visiting family, which has given me a broader perspective on how A.A. functions in different cultural contexts.

In summary, I bring a combination of long-term sobriety, extensive A.A. service experience, nonprofit governance and management skills, financial literacy, and a personal commitment to inclusivity. I am comfortable with complexity and ambiguity, and I try to approach all service work with humility, willingness to listen, and a focus on what best serves the still-suffering alcoholic.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Current Or Past Contractual Relationships With Aa World Services Inc Aa Grapevine Inc Or Aa General Service Board Inc
answers.value:
I have no current or past contractual relationships with A.A. World Services, Inc., A.A. Grapevine, Inc., or the A.A. General Service Board, Inc.

My interactions with these entities have been strictly in the context of A.A. service and participation, including:
- Ordering literature and materials for my home group, district, and area through normal channels, paying standard prices with group or district funds.
- Communicating with G.S.O. staff as an Area Delegate (Panel 70) regarding Conference background material, reports, and routine service questions.
- Participating in Regional Forums and service events where trustees, A.A.W.S. directors, and G.S.O. staff were present, always as a non-paid, voluntary A.A. member.

I have never received any honoraria, fees, or compensation from A.A. World Services, A.A. Grapevine, or the General Service Board, nor have I entered into any consulting, publishing, or employment contracts with them. I understand the importance of avoiding conflicts of interest and maintaining A.A.’s traditions of self-support and non-affiliation.
